_notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.name: WEC Energy Group, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
company_profile.description: WEC Energy Group, Inc., through its subsidiaries, provides regulated natural gas and electricity, and renewable and nonregulated renewable energy services in the United States. The company operates through Wisconsin, Illinois, Other States, Electric Transmission, and Non-Utility Energy Infrastructure segments. It generates and distributes electricity from coal, natural gas, oil, and nuclear, as well as renewable energy resources, including wind, solar, hydroelectric, and biomass; and distributes and hydroelectric natural gas. The company also owns, maintains, monitors, and operates electric transmission systems; and generates, distributes, and sells steam. As of December 31, 2025, the company operated approximately 35,200 miles of overhead distribution lines and 37,600 miles of underground distribution cables, as well as 420 electric distribution substations and 649,500 line transformers; approximately 47,200 miles of natural gas distribution mains; 1,300 miles of natural gas transmission mains; 2.4 million natural gas lateral services; 510 natural gas distribution and transmission gate stations; and 67.0 billion cubic feet of working gas capacities in underground natural gas storage fields. The company was formerly known as Wisconsin Energy Corporation and changed its name to WEC Energy Group, Inc. in June 2015. WEC Energy Group, Inc. was founded in 1896 and is headquartered in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.
company_profile.description_summary:
Provides regulated natural gas and electricity across the United States through multiple subsidiaries and segments. Generates electricity from coal, natural gas, nuclear, and renewable sources including wind and solar, while distributing natural gas and operating electric transmission systems. Maintains extensive infrastructure including over 35,000 miles of distribution lines and substantial natural gas storage capacity.

The company operates across Wisconsin, Illinois, and other states with a non-utility renewable energy segment, distinguishing it through diversified geographic presence and integrated renewable energy operations beyond traditional utility services.

_meta.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
_meta.method_explanation: Utilities like electricity and water companies operate under government regulation, which means their prices and profits are capped but also very stable. They tend to carry a lot of debt because building infrastructure is expensive — but this is normal and expected for the industry. Because earnings are so predictable, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and EV/EBITDA work well. DCF is less useful here because regulated utilities have limited ability to grow beyond what regulators allow.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: WEC Energy has a moderate moat rooted in regulated utility operations and predictable cash flows, but weak returns on invested capital (4.81%) and high leverage undermine competitive durability.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 16.2% profit margin is solid for a utility and shows the business extracts reasonable earnings from every dollar of revenue—typically utilities range from 12–18%, so this sits in the upper half of that band. WEC is managing its cost base effectively and converting revenue into profit reliably. However, a 11.68% return on equity is pedestrian; most quality businesses target 15%+ ROE, meaning WEC is not generating outsized returns on shareholder capital despite stable demand for its services.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 0.59 signals immediate liquidity stress—healthy utilities typically maintain 0.8–1.2—and a debt-to-EBITDA of 5.68x is elevated for the sector (3.5–4.5x is normal), meaning the company carries roughly 5.7 years of operating cash flow in debt. While utilities can service debt from regulated cash flows, this leverage constrains financial flexibility and limits the company's ability to weather unexpected shocks or invest aggressively without refinancing.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 9% is respectable for a mature utility and suggests some expansion from customer growth or rate adjustments, but projected EPS growth of 7.2% over five years lags both revenue and inflation, indicating earnings are not keeping pace with top-line expansion. The company's growth is constrained, which means regulatory caps, rising operational costs, or capital intensity are eating into profit expansion—typical of regulated utilities but still a limiting factor for total shareholder returns.
metric_assessments.predictability: Utilities are inherently predictable due to regulated rates and essential services, and WEC's 4/10 score reflects that structural advantage; however, a score of 4 rather than 7+ suggests regulatory or operational uncertainties are dampening confidence. High leverage and tight liquidity create earnings volatility risk if rates are not adjusted in line with costs, or if capital requirements exceed cash generation, making near-term results less certain than they should be for a regulated monopoly.
metric_assessments.valuation: A 4/10 valuation score indicates the stock is neither cheap nor expensive—likely trading near historical averages or fair value given the utility sector's modest growth and stable yields. With 7.2% expected earnings growth and a constrained growth profile, the stock is pricing in the company's regulated utility nature but offering limited upside surprise potential; investors are paying a fair price for a slow, stable business with above-average debt.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: WEC Energy is rated Dangerous because its financial strength is critically weak: the current ratio of 0.59 and debt-to-EBITDA of 5.68x create refinancing and liquidity risks that could strain the business if interest rates rise or economic conditions soften. While the company's profitability and regulated cash flows help it service debt today, the combination of high leverage, weak returns on capital (4.81% ROIC), and constrained growth leaves little margin for error or unexpected challenges—typical of utilities that have overleveraged to fund infrastructure or dividends.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: WEC Energy is a textbook regulated utility: it generates steady profits and serves an essential need, with a solid 16.2% profit margin showing competent operations, but the business is burdened by excessive debt and weak returns that cap long-term value creation. The main risk is financial vulnerability—a current ratio near 0.6 and debt nearly 5.7x operating cash flow leave limited cushion if interest rates spike or capital spending needs accelerate, a genuine concern for an investor seeking stability. Growth is uninspiring at 7.2% EPS expansion over five years, constrained by regulation and rising costs, meaning you are betting on steady dividends rather than meaningful stock appreciation. At current valuation (trading at fair value, not cheap), WEC offers modest yield and predictability but demands acceptance of high financial risk for returns that don't meaningfully exceed broader market growth. Unless you have high risk tolerance and confidence in utility sector rate relief, this is best approached as a hold-only position rather than a new buy.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: WEC Energy Group, Inc. demonstrates weak fundamental quality and is constrained by financial strength.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: Key risks to watch: elevated leverage (Debt/EBITDA 5.7x); tight liquidity (current ratio 0.59); balance sheet constraints on growth.
quality_narrative.investment_summary: With 7.2% consensus EPS growth forecast, the growth outlook adds some uncertainty to the investment case. The stock trades near estimated intrinsic value and is warrants caution — careful position sizing and ongoing monitoring are essential.

company_profile.description: Winnebago Industries, Inc. manufactures and sells recreation outdoor lifestyle products primarily for use in leisure travel and outdoor recreation activities. It operates through three segments: Towable RV, Motorhome RV, and Marine. The company provides towable products that are non-motorized vehicles to be towed by automobiles, pickup trucks, SUVs, or vans for use as temporary living quarters, such as conventional travel trailers, fifth wheels, folding camper trailers, truck campers, and park models under the Winnebago and Grand Design brand names. It also offers motorhome RV, a self-propelled mobile dwelling used primarily as temporary living quarters during vacation and camping trips, or to support active and mobile lifestyles under the Winnebago, Newmar, and Grand Design brand names. In addition, the company offers other specialty commercial vehicles for law enforcement command centers, mobile medical clinics, and mobile office spaces; commercial vehicles as bare shells to third-party up fitters, as well as manufactures and sells recreational boats under the Chris-Craft and Barletta brand names. Further, it is involved in the original equipment manufacturing of parts for other manufacturers and commercial vehicles. The company sells its products primarily through independent dealers in the United States, Canada, and internationally. Winnebago Industries, Inc. was incorporated in 1958 and is based in Eden Prairie, Minnesota.
company_profile.description_summary:
Manufactures and sells recreational vehicles and boats for leisure travel and outdoor recreation. It also manufactures specialty commercial vehicles and parts for other manufacturers.

The company distinguishes itself through diverse product offerings spanning multiple recreation categories and specialized commercial applications, alongside original equipment manufacturing services for third parties.

company_profile.name: Cactus, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: Cactus, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, designs, manufactures, sells, and rents engineered pressure control and spoolable pipe technologies in the United States, Australia, Canada, the Middle East, and internationally. The company operates in two segments: Pressure Control and Spoolable Technologies. The Pressure Control segment designs, manufactures, sells, and rents a range of wellheads and pressure control equipment under the Cactus Wellhead brand through its service centers. Its products are sold and rented primarily for onshore unconventional oil and gas wells for drilling, completion, and production phases. This segment also offers field services for its products and rental items to assist with the installation, maintenance, and handling of the equipment. The Spoolable Technologies segment designs, manufactures, and sells spoolable pipes and associated end fittings under the FlexSteel brand. Its products are primarily used in production, gathering, and takeaway pipelines to transport oil, gas, and other liquids. This segment also provides field services and rental items to assist with installation through service centers and pipe yards, as well as equipment and services. The company also offers repair and refurbishment services for pressure control equipment. Cactus, Inc. was founded in 2011 and is headquartered in Houston, Texas.
company_profile.description_summary:
Cactus designs, manufactures, and rents pressure control equipment and spoolable pipe technologies for the oil and gas industry. It operates two main segments: Pressure Control, which produces wellheads and pressure equipment primarily for onshore unconventional wells, and Spoolable Technologies, which manufactures flexible pipes for production and transportation pipelines. Both segments provide field services and rental options.

The company distinguishes itself through specialized brands—Cactus Wellhead and FlexSteel—and comprehensive support services including installation, maintenance, and equipment refurbishment across multiple geographic markets.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

_meta.type_rationale: Bank or thrift — P/Tangible Book prioritised; DCF excluded
_meta.method_explanation: Banks make money from the difference between what they charge on loans and what they pay on deposits — not from traditional business cash flows. Because of this, standard cash flow models (DCF) do not work well for banks. Instead, we focus on Price-to-Book (P/B), which compares the stock price to the value of the bank's assets on its books. Banks also carry a lot of debt by design — they borrow money to lend it out — so high leverage is normal and not a red flag.

quality.company_type_note: Banks and REITs carry structural leverage — do not describe financial_strength scores of 7+ as 'moderate concern' for these types.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Wells Fargo's exceptional predictability, strong profitability (12% ROE), and dominant market position as a nationwide bank create a durable competitive moat, though modest growth and regulatory constraints prevent it from reaching the highest tier.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 25.5% profit margin is exceptional for a bank and well above the typical 15–20% range, showing the company extracts strong earnings from every dollar of revenue. This reflects efficient operations, effective cost control, and solid pricing power in lending and deposits. However, the margin hasn't expanded further despite scale advantages, suggesting limited room to improve profitability through operational leverage alone.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: With an 8/10 score, Wells Fargo maintains the capital and earnings cushion expected of a systemically important bank, and the 12% ROE demonstrates it generates reasonable returns on shareholder equity. The strong rating reflects the company's ability to weather economic downturns and meet regulatory requirements, which is the foundation of banking safety. The company's size and diversified revenue streams provide stability, though it remains subject to sector-wide regulatory pressures that cap how aggressively it can deploy capital.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue growth of 5.7% and projected EPS growth of 13.1% over five years represent solid, above-average expansion, yet the 4/10 score reflects that these rates lag both the broader market and peer growth in a recovering economy. The faster EPS growth versus revenue growth signals the company is harvesting efficiency gains and buybacks, not building new business at scale. Without stronger organic growth or market share expansion, Wells Fargo remains a mature business generating returns for existing shareholders rather than creating new wealth through expansion.
metric_assessments.predictability: The perfect 10/10 score reflects that banking earnings are highly stable and forecastable—interest rates, loan volumes, and deposit bases don't swing wildly month to month, and Wells Fargo's diversified customer base dampens idiosyncratic risk. Regulators also provide transparency into capital adequacy and stress-test results, making future performance easier for investors to model. This predictability is exactly what makes banks useful portfolio holdings during uncertain times, though it comes with the trade-off of lower upside surprise potential.
metric_assessments.valuation: A 6/10 valuation score indicates the stock is priced fairly relative to its cash generation and earnings, neither compelling nor overpriced. At current levels, investors are paying a reasonable but not discounted price for the stable cash flows and 12% ROE the company delivers, which is appropriate for a mature financial institution. The valuation leaves little room for disappointment, meaning price upside will depend on either unexpected earnings growth or multiple expansion—neither of which appears likely in the near term.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Wells Fargo earned a Safe rating because it combines rock-solid financial strength (8/10), exceptional predictability (10/10), and strong profitability (8/10) that make it unlikely to disappoint investors seeking stability and reliable dividends. In practice, this means you're buying a dependable, low-drama bank that will likely deliver steady returns without spectacular surprises—ideal for conservative investors but not for those hunting outsized gains.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Wells Fargo is a fortress bank that earns exceptional profits (25.5% margins and 12% ROE) with virtually bulletproof predictability, making it one of the most reliable financial institutions an investor can own. The company's main limitation is that growth is modest at 5.7% revenue expansion and 13.1% projected EPS growth—respectable but not exciting in an era when the broader economy and markets offer faster-growing alternatives. At current valuation levels (6/10 score), the stock is fairly priced, meaning you're not getting a bargain, but you're also not overpaying for stability and dividends. This is a hold-and-collect income play for investors who prioritize capital preservation and steady returns over the chance to capture rapid growth; Wells Fargo rewards patience with its shareholders, not adrenaline.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

quality.note: Moat scored by Haiku API — final_score_pct computed out of 50.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Whirlpool has virtually no competitive moat; its razor-thin 1.1% profit margin, anemic 5.78% return on invested capital, and declining revenues indicate the company competes primarily on price in a commoditized appliance market with limited pricing power or customer loyalty.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 1.1% profit margin means Whirlpool keeps just 1.1 cents of profit for every dollar of sales—far below the 5–8% range typical of healthy industrial manufacturers, and more comparable to struggling discount retailers. The company is technically profitable, which prevents a zero score, but the margin is so thin that any cost spike or competitive pressure quickly erodes earnings. What's holding this back is the highly commoditized nature of the appliance business, where consumers see little differentiation between brands and shop heavily on price alone.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: With a current ratio of 0.76, Whirlpool has only $0.76 in liquid assets for every $1.00 of near-term obligations—a red flag indicating tight short-term cash flexibility, well below the 1.0–1.5 range investors expect from stable companies. More alarming, a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 7.86 means the company would need nearly 8 years of all its operating profits just to pay down debt, far exceeding the 2–3 times that lenders and investors consider manageable. The business is overleveraged relative to its earnings capacity, leaving little room for downturns or unexpected expenses.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue fell 9.6% year-over-year, and the company is growing earnings at just 2.1% annually over the next five years—essentially flat after inflation—signaling a mature, stagnant business with no meaningful momentum. Management has flagged that growth is constrained, suggesting structural headwinds such as market saturation, weak housing demand, or price competition that cannot be easily overcome. Without revenue acceleration and with margins already razor-thin, the path to stronger earnings is severely limited.
metric_assessments.predictability: A 2/10 score reflects a combination of volatile revenues (shrinking 9.6%), a razor-thin profit margin vulnerable to cost swings, and weak operational metrics (low ROIC, high leverage) that make future earnings hard to forecast reliably. Whirlpool's performance depends heavily on consumer discretionary spending tied to housing, which fluctuates with economic cycles, and the company lacks brand pricing power or operational leverage to cushion downturns. The weak underlying business model makes it difficult for investors to predict whether earnings will stabilize, shrink further, or recover.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a 10/10 valuation score, the stock appears cheap on traditional price-to-earnings or price-to-book metrics, reflecting the market's justified concerns about the weak business fundamentals and leverage load. However, a cheap valuation is only attractive if the business can recover or maintain stable earnings; Whirlpool's deteriorating revenues, thin margins, and high debt burden suggest the low price may be a warning rather than an opportunity. The valuation is excellent only for a contrarian bet that management can turn around operations—a high-risk proposition given the structural headwinds in appliance demand.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Whirlpool is rated Dangerous because it combines a deteriorating core business (revenues down 9.6%, profit margins barely above 1%) with excessive debt (7.86x EBITDA) and poor liquidity (current ratio of 0.76), leaving the company vulnerable to economic slowdowns, rising interest rates, or supply-chain shocks. The combination of operational weakness and financial leverage creates real risk that debt obligations could become unmanageable if earnings decline further, potentially forcing restructuring or significant equity dilution to shareholders.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Whirlpool is a mature appliance manufacturer facing a structurally challenging market: revenues are shrinking, profit margins are razor-thin at 1.1%, and return on capital is weak at 5.78%, suggesting the company struggles to compete on anything other than price. The bigger concern is the balance sheet: with debt nearly 8 times operating earnings and a current ratio of 0.76, the company has limited financial flexibility to weather a recession or invest in innovation, and high leverage leaves shareholders exposed if earnings deteriorate. Growth is essentially flat at 2% annually over the next five years with management explicitly stating growth is constrained, indicating the business faces structural headwinds unlikely to reverse soon. The stock does appear cheap by valuation metrics, but that reflects justified market skepticism about the company's ability to improve profitability or stabilize revenues while servicing its debt load. For most beginner investors, this is a stock to avoid: the low price is a warning sign, not a bargain.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: Whirlpool Corporation demonstrates weak fundamental quality and is constrained by financial strength.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: Key risks to watch: elevated leverage (Debt/EBITDA 7.9x); tight liquidity (current ratio 0.76); a weak near-term growth outlook.
quality_narrative.investment_summary: With 2.1% consensus EPS growth forecast, the growth outlook adds some uncertainty to the investment case. The stock trades at a 47% discount to estimated intrinsic value and is warrants caution — careful position sizing and ongoing monitoring are essential.

_meta.type_rationale: Real estate investment trust — P/NAV (P/B) prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) own properties and are legally required to pay out most of their income as dividends. _meta.type_rationale: Speculative growth — EPS fwd 40.7%, profitability 2/10; P/S and PSG prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: High-growth companies — typically in technology or biotech — are often not yet profitable, which means earnings-based methods like P/E give no useful result. Instead, we use Price-to-Sales (P/S), which compares the stock price to total revenue regardless of whether the company is making a profit. The PSG ratio (Price/Sales/Growth) also adjusts for how fast revenue is growing. These methods are imperfect but they are the best available tools when a company is still investing heavily to grow.

These include Discounted Cash Flow (which estimates the present value of future cash flows), earnings multiples (P/E and PEG), revenue multiples (P/S and PSG), book value (P/B), and EV/EBITDA. Each method approaches value from a different angle — using all of them together gives a more rounded estimate than relying on any single number.

_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. _notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.name: W&T Offshore, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: W&T Offshore, Inc., an independent oil and natural gas producer, engages in the acquisition, exploration, and development of oil and natural gas properties in the Gulf of America. The company sells crude oil, condensate, natural gas, liquids, and natural gas liquids. The company also provides construction, drilling, and production activities necessary to retrieve oil and gas from its natural reservoirs, including the acquisition, construction, installation, and maintenance of field gathering and storage systems, such as lifting oil and gas, and gathering, treating, and field processing; and extraction of hydrocarbons, solid, liquid, or gaseous state, oil sands, shale, coalbeds, or other nonrenewable natural resources. W&T Offshore, Inc. was founded in 1983 and is headquartered in Houston, Texas.
company_profile.description_summary:
An independent oil and natural gas producer, the company acquires, explores, and develops properties in the Gulf of America. It generates revenue by selling crude oil, condensate, natural gas, liquids, and natural gas liquids extracted from its reserves.

The company provides comprehensive upstream services including drilling, construction, production activities, and maintenance of field gathering and storage systems necessary to extract and process hydrocarbons.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.name: Essential Utilities, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
company_profile.description: Essential Utilities, Inc., through its subsidiaries, operates regulated utilities that provide water, wastewater, and natural gas services in the United States. The company operates through Regulated Water; and Regulated Natural Gas segments. It also provides utility service line protection solutions and repair services to households; gas marketing and production activities; and natural gas distribution services. The company serves approximately 5.5 million residential water, commercial water, fire protection, industrial water, wastewater, and other water and utility customers in Pennsylvania, Ohio, Texas, Illinois, North Carolina, New Jersey, Indiana, Virginia, and Kentucky under the Aqua and Peoples brands. The company was formerly known as Aqua America, Inc. and changed its name to Essential Utilities, Inc. in February 2020. Essential Utilities, Inc. was founded in 1886 and is headquartered in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania.
company_profile.description_summary:
This regulated utility company provides water, wastewater, and natural gas services across nine U.S. states, serving approximately 5.5 million customers under the Aqua and Peoples brands. Water and wastewater services represent its primary revenue source, supplemented by natural gas distribution operations.

The company also offers utility service line protection solutions and repair services to households, along with gas marketing and production activities, diversifying its revenue streams beyond core utility operations.

_meta.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
_meta.method_explanation: Utilities like electricity and water companies operate under government regulation, which means their prices and profits are capped but also very stable. They tend to carry a lot of debt because building infrastructure is expensive — but this is normal and expected for the industry. Because earnings are so predictable, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and EV/EBITDA work well. DCF is less useful here because regulated utilities have limited ability to grow beyond what regulators allow.
